waterClarity.plain: How deep into the water you can still see, measured by lowering a black-and-white Secchi disk until it disappears.
waterClarity.why: Clearer water generally means fewer algae and suspended particles, so it's more pleasant to swim in and a sign the lake is healthier.
waterClarity.normal: Lake Quinsigamond typically reads about 5-15 ft (1.5-4.5 m) in the open lake during summer; deeper (clearer) is better.

bands.label: Excellent
bands.note: Clear, oligotrophic-like water (general trophic guidance)

bands.label: Fair
bands.note: Worcester 'State of the Lake' rated clarity 'Fair' in 2023 when most readings fell 4-10 ft

bands.label: Poor
bands.note: Below MA turbidity-impairment threshold; lake is listed impaired when summer Secchi falls below ~4 ft

waterClarity.source: Worcester 'State of the Lake' Excellent/Good/Fair/Poor bands; MA turbidity impairment (MassDEP 303(d), Secchi ~4 ft / 1.2 m); general lake trophic-state guidance (EPA / NALMS-Carlson)

waterTemperature.plain: How warm the lake's surface water is.
waterTemperature.why: It sets how comfortable swimming feels and, when it gets too warm, it stresses cold-water fish and helps fuel algae blooms.
waterTemperature.normal: Comfortable summer swimming is roughly 70-82°F (21-28°C); cold-water fish (e.g., trout) begin to stress above about 68°F (20°C).

bands.label: Cold-water-fish stress
bands.note: Approximate; species-dependent. MA warm-water-fishery criterion generally caps at 83-86°F (28.3-30°C)

waterTemperature.source: MassDEP Surface Water Quality Standards (314 CMR 4.00) temperature criteria; general aquatic-life and recreation guidance (EPA). Comfort ranges are general guidance, not a regulatory threshold.

dissolvedOxygen.plain: The amount of oxygen dissolved in the water that fish and other aquatic life need to breathe.
dissolvedOxygen.why: Low oxygen suffocates fish and signals pollution or decaying algae; healthy oxygen means a living, balanced lake.
dissolvedOxygen.normal: Healthy surface water is about 5-6+ mg/L (often higher when cool or windy); near 0 mg/L is a 'dead zone'.

bands.label: Adequate
bands.note: MA warm-water-fishery minimum is 5.0 mg/L; cold-water fisheries 6.0 mg/L

bands.label: Stressful / hypoxic
bands.note: Fish stress; below ~4 mg/L is widely used as the hypoxia warning threshold

bands.label: Dead zone
bands.note: Anoxic; lethal to most aquatic life

dissolvedOxygen.source: MassDEP Surface Water Quality Standards (314 CMR 4.00): 5.0 mg/L (warm-water), 6.0 mg/L (cold-water) minimums; EPA aquatic-life guidance. Lake bottom waters often drop low naturally when stratified in summer.

pH.plain: How acidic or basic the water is, on a 0-14 scale where 7 is neutral.
pH.why: Water that is too acidic or too basic harms fish and aquatic life and can swing during heavy algae blooms.
pH.normal: Healthy lake water sits between 6.5 and 8.5 standard units.

bands.label: Too basic
bands.note: High daytime pH can occur during heavy algae/cyanobacteria blooms

pH.source: MassDEP Surface Water Quality Standards (314 CMR 4.00) / EPA: 6.5-8.5 SU for aquatic life and recreation.

totalPhosphorus.plain: The total amount of phosphorus, a plant nutrient, dissolved and attached to particles in the water.
totalPhosphorus.why: Phosphorus is the main fuel for algae and cyanobacteria blooms, so low surface levels mean less bloom risk.
totalPhosphorus.normal: Surface phosphorus below 0.025 mg/L (25 µg/L) is 'Excellent' in Worcester's reports; EPA flags lakes as eutrophic well above this.

bands.label: Excellent
bands.note: Worcester surface-TP 'Excellent' threshold; surface TP at Quinsigamond is usually here

bands.label: Mesotrophic
bands.note: General trophic guidance

bands.label: Eutrophic (bloom-prone)
bands.note: EPA often cites ~0.020 mg/L total P as a guidance level to limit algal nuisance in lakes

bands.label: Hypereutrophic
bands.note: Deep/bottom waters at Quinsigamond rise far above this late in summer (0.3-0.7 mg/L), but surface is what bathers contact

totalPhosphorus.source: Worcester 'State of the Lake' surface-TP 'Excellent' < 0.025 mg/L; EPA Quality Criteria for Water (~0.020 mg/L total P to control eutrophication; ~0.050 mg/L where streams enter lakes) and lake trophic-state context.

cyanobacteria.plain: Microscopic blue-green algae that can multiply into scummy blooms, measured by the pigment phycocyanin (µg/L) or by counting cells (cells/mL).
cyanobacteria.why: Some cyanobacteria release toxins that can sicken swimmers, pets, and children, so blooms can close a beach.
cyanobacteria.normal: Phycocyanin staying below ~50 µg/L and cell counts well under 70,000 cells/mL indicate no bloom advisory.

bands.label: Bloom indicator (watch)
bands.note: Bloom-indicator threshold used by Worcester / WCMC monitoring

bands.label: Advisory threshold
bands.note: MA DPH posts a recreational advisory at this cell density (≈14 µg/L microcystin); also issued on visible scum/toxin

bands.label: Avoid contact
bands.note: Keep people and pets out of the water

cyanobacteria.source: Worcester County / WCMC phycocyanin bloom indicator (~50 µg/L); MA DPH 'Guidelines for Cyanobacteria at Recreational Freshwater Locations' (advisory at 70,000 cells/mL or visible scum/toxin), based on US EPA / WHO recommendations.

turbidity.plain: How cloudy or murky the water is from suspended particles like sediment and algae, measured in NTU.
turbidity.why: Cloudier water looks less inviting, can carry pollutants, and often goes hand-in-hand with poorer clarity and more algae.
turbidity.normal: Clear lake water is usually under ~5 NTU; the higher the number, the murkier the water.

bands.label: Cloudy / turbid
bands.note: Often spikes after heavy rain and runoff

turbidity.source: General guidance based on EPA recreational-water and drinking-water context; MassDEP assesses turbidity impairment via clarity (Secchi) rather than a single statewide NTU recreation limit, so these NTU bands are general guidance.

chlorophyllA.plain: The green pigment in algae, used as a stand-in for how much algae is growing in the water (µg/L).
chlorophyllA.why: More chlorophyll means more algae, which clouds the water and raises the odds of a nuisance or harmful bloom.
chlorophyllA.normal: Low-algae lakes run under ~7 µg/L; Quinsigamond's open water is usually in the low single digits to low teens.

bands.label: Eutrophic
bands.note: EPA often cites ~10-12 µg/L as a guidance level to limit nuisance algae

chlorophyllA.source: EPA / NALMS-Carlson trophic-state index and EPA nutrient-criteria guidance for lakes and reservoirs (general trophic guidance).

specificConductance.plain: How well the water conducts electricity, which rises with dissolved salts and minerals (µS/cm at 25°C).
specificConductance.why: In this region a high or rising value is a red flag for road-salt pollution from winter de-icing washing into the lake.
specificConductance.normal: Unimpacted New England lakes often run well under ~250 µS/cm; urban lakes like Quinsigamond read higher due to road salt.

bands.label: High (chloride concern)
bands.note: Roughly corresponds to chloride nearing EPA's 230 mg/L chronic aquatic-life criterion; exact conversion varies

specificConductance.source: EPA chloride aquatic-life criteria (230 mg/L chronic, 860 mg/L acute) as the regulatory anchor; conductivity bands are general regional guidance (no single statewide µS/cm limit), since conductivity is an indicator, not itself a standard.

nitrate.plain: A dissolved form of nitrogen, another plant nutrient that comes from fertilizer, septic systems, and runoff (mg/L).
nitrate.why: Extra nitrogen feeds algae growth, and very high nitrate is a drinking-water health concern, so lower is better.
nitrate.normal: Clean lake surface water is typically well under 1 mg/L nitrate-N; the drinking-water limit is 10 mg/L.

bands.label: Drinking-water limit
bands.note: EPA Maximum Contaminant Level for drinking water; lakes rarely approach this

nitrate.source: EPA National Primary Drinking Water Regulation nitrate MCL = 10 mg/L (as N); lower bands are general lake-nutrient guidance (recreational lakes have no single nitrate limit).

beachBacteria.plain: A fecal-indicator bacteria counted in the water to flag possible sewage or animal-waste contamination (CFU or MPN per 100 mL).
beachBacteria.why: High counts mean a higher chance of getting sick from swimming, and they are what trigger a beach closure.
beachBacteria.normal: Safe swimming is a single sample at or below 61 CFU/100 mL and a 5-sample geometric mean at or below 33 CFU/100 mL.

bands.label: Safe (geometric mean)
bands.note: Rolling geomean of recent samples

bands.label: Beach posting / closure
bands.note: Beach is posted/closed until resampling passes

beachBacteria.source: Massachusetts DPH bathing-beach standard for freshwater (105 CMR 445.000): Enterococci single-sample 61 CFU/100 mL, geometric mean 33 CFU/100 mL, based on EPA Recreational Water Quality Criteria.

riverFlow.term: River flow at the lake outlet (USGS 01110000)
riverFlow.plain: How much water per second is flowing in the Quinsigamond River as it leaves the lake, measured in cubic feet per second (cfs).
riverFlow.why: It rises and falls with rain and runoff, giving a quick read on storm response, but it is the river leaving the lake, not the lake's water level.
riverFlow.normal: Varies seasonally; low/baseflow in dry summer weather and sharp spikes after storms. This is a context signal, not a good/bad score.

bands.label: Baseflow / dry
bands.note: Typical dry-weather flow

bands.label: Storm response
bands.note: Spikes after rain; often coincides with runoff, higher turbidity and bacteria

riverFlow.source: USGS National Water Information System, Quinsigamond River station 01110000 (Quinsigamond River at North Grafton, MA), the outlet downstream of the lake. Note: this is river discharge LEAVING the lake, a storm-response proxy, NOT the lake's water elevation. No regulatory good/bad threshold.
